23: David's declaration to Shimei: lŏʼ tāmūt — 'you shall not die.' The three-word royal pardon is the covenantal counterpart to the death sentence: just as the king can decree môt yāmūt (he shall surely die), the king can speak lŏʼ tāmūt (you shall not die). The formula appears in YHWH's direct pardon speech: in 2 Sam 12:13 Nathan tells David gam YHWH heʿeăbír ḥaṭṭāʾẑḿkā lŏʼ tāmūt ('YHWH has put away your sin; you shall not die'), making the royal pardon of Shimei a deliberate echo of the divine pardon of David. The king who was himself pardoned by the divine king now extends pardon to one who deserved death. This is the OT narrative pattern of the pardoned pardoner — the pattern that underlies the Parable of the Unforgiving Servant (Matt 18:23-35): the servant who was forgiven ten thousand talents and then refused to forgive a hundred denarii has violated the logic of the lŏʼ tāmūt economy. David's pardon of Shimei demonstrates the opposite: the recipient of royal grace extending royal grace.
1: Va-yuggad le-Yoav: hinneh ha-melekh bokheh va-yit'abbel al-Avshalom — “And it was told to Joab: behold, the king is weeping and mourning for Absalom.” The Hophal passive yuggad (it was told) maintains narrator anonymity about the informant. Bokheh va-yit'abbel (weeping and mourning) combines the physical act (bakhah, cry) with the formal ritual state (evel, mourning) — David is in grief and in the formal state of one observing mourning simultaneously.
2: Va-tehi ha-teshu'ah ba-yom ha-hu le-evel — “And the victory on that day was turned into mourning.” The wordplay of teshu'ah (victory, deliverance) and evel (mourning) in the same verse expresses the day's paradox: military success and personal catastrophe simultaneously. Ki-shama ha-am ba-yom ha-hu lemor: nit'atzev ha-melekh al-beno — the people heard that the king was grieving (nit'atzev, Niphal of atsav, to be pained, grieved) for his son.
3: Va-yitganev ha-am ba-yom ha-hu la-vo ha-ir — “And the people stole (hitganev, Hithpael of ganav) into the city that day.” The Hithpael of ganav — the same root used for Absalom “stealing the hearts” of Israel (15:6) — now describes the victorious army slipping into the city like defeated men. The semantic inversion is pointed: the army that should enter in triumph enters in shame. Ka-asher yitganev ha-am hanikklamim be-nusam ba-milchamah — as people who are ashamed and have fled in battle sneak away.
4: Ve-ha-melekh kissa et-panav — “And the king covered his face.” The gesture of covering the face (kassa et-panav) is the posture of grief that withdraws from public view. Va-yiz'aq ha-melekh qol gadol: beni Avshalom, Avshalom beni beni — the repeated cry beni (my son) — five times in this verse and the preceding chapter — encodes grief in repeated grammatical units that resist rational processing.
5: Vayavo Yoav el-ha-melekh ha-bayit vayomer — Joab's confrontation with David. His speech (vv. 5–7) is the bluntest address to a king in the Succession Narrative. Hevoshta ha-yom et-penei kol-avadekhah — “you have today shamed the faces of all your servants.” Hevoshta (Hiphil of bosh, to be ashamed/put to shame) — to shame another's face was to publicly negate their honor, the most serious social harm in ancient Near Eastern culture.
6: Le-ahavah et-sone'ekha ule-sno et-ohavekhah — “by loving those who hate you and hating those who love you.” Joab's chiastic accusation expresses the inversion of proper relational logic: David loves his enemies (Absalom who tried to kill him) and hates his friends (the army that saved him). Ki higgadta ha-yom ki ein-lekha sarim va-avadim — “for you have declared today that commanders and servants are nothing to you.”
7: Ve-attah qum tze ve-dabber al-lev avadekhah — Joab's command to David: “rise, go out and speak to the heart (al-lev) of your servants.” The phrase dibber al-lev (speak to the heart) is the idiom for comfort and reassurance — it appears in Gen 50:21 (Joseph comforting his brothers), Isa 40:2 (YHWH comforting Israel), and Ruth 2:13. Joab frames a political ultimatum in the rhetoric of pastoral care.
8: Va-yaqom ha-melekh vayeshev ba-sha'ar — David's obedience: he rises and sits in the gate (sha'ar) — the judicial-civic space. Va-yagidu le-khol-ha-am lemor: hinneh ha-melekh yoshev ba-sha'ar — the announcement of the king's presence in the gate draws all the people before him. The gate-session is the king's performance of his judicial role — exactly the role Absalom had usurped in 15:2.
9: Ve-khol-ha-am hayah nadan be-khol shivtei Yisra'el lemor — “And all the people were disputing among all the tribes of Israel.” Nadan (Niphal participle of don/din, to quarrel, contend) — the tribes in contention with one another. The dispute (vv. 9–15) is the formal return-negotiation between David and the tribal leadership — a political process, not merely a personal restoration.
10: Ve-Avshalom asher mashachnu alenu met ba-milchamah — the people's acknowledgment that Absalom, whom they anointed (mashachnu, Qal of mashach, anoint) over themselves, is dead in battle. Ve-attah lammah attem machasherim lehashiv et-ha-melekh — “and now why are you silent about bringing the king back?” Machasherim (Hiphil participle of charash, be silent) — the accusation that the tribal leadership was slow to act on the political necessity.
11: Ve-ha-melekh David shalach el-Tzadoq ve-el-Evyatar ha-kohanim lemor — David's direct outreach to the Judahite elders through the priests. Dibberu el-ziqnei Yehudah lemor: maddua tihyu acharonim le-hashiv et-ha-melekh el-beito — “why are you last to bring the king back to his house?” David appeals to tribal pride: Judah, his own tribe, should be leading the restoration, not following.
12: Achai attem atzmai u-vesari attem — “you are my brothers; you are my bone and my flesh.” The kinship formula atzmi u-vesari (my bone and my flesh) is the language of covenant kinship — it appears in Gen 2:23 (Adam's recognition of Eve), Gen 29:14 (Laban's recognition of Jacob), and Judg 9:2. David deploys the tribal bond as the basis for Judah's special obligation.
13: Ve-le-Amasa tomeru: halo atzmi u-vesari attah — David's offer to Amasa: “are you not my bone and my flesh?” — the kinship formula applied to Absalom's former general (David's nephew). The appointment of Amasa to replace Joab (koh ya'aseh li elohim ve-kho yosif im-lo tihyeh sar-tzava lefanai kol-ha-yamim tachat Yoav — may God do so to me if you are not commander before me instead of Joab) is politically calculated: winning over Absalom's former commander consolidates the restoration while punishing Joab for killing Absalom against David's command.
14: Va-yat levav kol-ish Yehudah ke-ish echad — “And he bent the heart of all the men of Judah as one man.” Vayat (Hiphil of natah, to incline, bend) — David's outreach bent the hearts — a deliberate contrast with Absalom's vayignov (stealing) of hearts (15:6). David uses legitimate persuasion through kinship appeal; Absalom used deceptive seduction. Ke-ish echad (as one man) is the formula of unified collective resolve.
15: Va-yavo ha-melekh ad-ha-Yarden — David's arrival at the Jordan for the formal return crossing. The Jordan-crossing in both directions frames the exile narrative: crossing east was departure (15:23), crossing back west is restoration. Ve-Yehudah ba le-Gilgal la-lekhet liqrat ha-melekh — Judah came to Gilgal to escort the king. Gilgal was the ancient entry-point to the land (Josh 4–5) — the first camp after Jordan-crossing. The choice of Gilgal as meeting-place invokes the conquest entry as a template for David's return.
16: Va-yemaher Shim'i ben-Gera ben-ha-Yemini asher mi-Bachurim — Shimei's rapid descent. Vayemaher (Piel of mahar, hurry) signals his awareness of danger. Ve-itto eleph ish mi-Vinyamin — a thousand Benjaminite men with him. The number signals political weight: Shimei arrives not alone but with a tribal contingent, framing his submission as a representative act.
17: Ve-Tziva na'ar beit Sha'ul ve-chamisha esrei banav ve-esrim avadav itto — Ziba with fifteen sons and twenty servants. Ziba's presence at the return-crossing alongside Shimei creates a confrontation that will force the Mephibosheth question back onto David's agenda.
18: Va-ya'avru ha-neshel la-a'avir et-beit ha-melekh — they crossed the ford (neshel, a shallow crossing point) to transport the royal household. Shimei fell before the king as he was about to cross the Jordan, prostrating himself in the petition posture.
19: Vayomer el-ha-melekh: al-yachshov li adoni avon — “Let not my lord the king reckon iniquity to me.” Al-yachshov (jussive of chashav, reckon, impute) with the negative is the standard formula for requesting non-imputation of guilt — the conceptual vocabulary of Ps 32:2 (lo yachshov-lo YHWH avon). Al-tizkor asher he'ava avdekha — do not remember (zakar) what your servant did wrong. The pairing of chashav (impute) and zakar (remember) covers both legal accounting and personal memory.
20: Ki yada avdekha ki anokhi chatati — “for your servant knows that I have sinned.” Yada here is not merely intellectual acknowledgment but recognized moral knowledge that carries legal force. Shimei's admission of sin (chatati) is the formal confession that activates the possibility of royal forgiveness. Ve-hinneh bati ha-yom rishon le-khol-beit Yosef la-redet liqrat adoni ha-melekh — being the first of the house of Joseph (northern tribes) to come down to meet the king.
21: Vaya'an Avishai ben-Tzeruyah vayomer: hatahat zot lo yumat Shim'i ki qillel et-meshiach YHWH — Abishai's demand for Shimei's execution: meshiach YHWH (anointed of YHWH) — the theological designation of the king as YHWH's anointed one whose person is sacred. Cursing the meshiach YHWH was a capital offense under ancient Israelite law; Abishai's citation is legally correct, even as David overrides it.
22: Vayomer David: mah-li va-lakhem benei Tzeruyah ki-tihyu li ha-yom le-satan — David's rebuke: le-satan (as an adversary/accuser) — satan as a legal adversary who brings accusations before a court. This is not the later cosmic adversary but its legal root. Ha-yom le-satan (as an adversary today) signals that on the day of the king's return, executing Shimei would inaugurate the restored reign with blood, not mercy.
24: U-Mephiboshet ben-Sha'ul yarad liqrat ha-melekh — Mephibosheth's descent to meet the king. Ve-lo asah raglav ve-lo asah sephato ve-et-begadav lo kibbes mi-yom lekhet ha-melekh ad-ha-yom asher-ba be-shalom — he had not dressed his feet (raglav), not trimmed his beard (sephato), and not washed his clothes (kibbes) from the day the king left until he returned in peace. The three neglected personal-care acts are the marks of someone observing the full mourning state for David's exile.
25: Va-yehi ki-va Yerushalaim liqrat ha-melekh vayomer lo ha-melekh: lammah lo-halahhta itti Mephiboshet — David's direct question: why did you not go with me? Lammah lo-halakhta itti (why did you not go with me?) is the same loyalty-test question Absalom asked Hushai (16:17) — testing whether the failure of accompaniment was choice or circumstance.
26: Vayomer: adoni ha-melekh avdi rimmani — “My lord the king, my servant deceived me.” Rimmani (Piel perfect of ramah, deceive, trick) — the same verb used for Jacob's deception of Isaac. Mephibosheth's counter-narrative: Ziba deceived him. Ki amar avdekha achboshshah-li ha-chamor ve-erkav alav ve-elekh el-ha-melekh ki pissea avdekha — he requested a donkey to ride because he is lame (pissea), but Ziba did not saddle it.
27: Va-yeshlel bi be-avdekha el-adoni ha-melekh — “And he slandered me (yeshlel, from ragal, to slander, go about as a spy) to my lord the king.” Va-adoni ha-melekh ke-malakh ha-elohim — the comparison of the king to an angel of God (also at 14:17; 16:17) places the burden of discernment on David's reputed wisdom.
28: Ki kol-beit avi lo-hayu ki im-anshei-mavet la-adoni ha-melekh — “all my father's house were nothing but dead men before my lord the king.” Anshei-mavet (men of death/doomed men) — all of Saul's household would have been executed except for David's chesed. Va-tasem et-avdekha be-toch okhlei shulchanekha — and you set your servant among those who eat at your table — the covenant adoption formula of ch. 9 invoked again.
29: Vayomer lo ha-melekh: lammah tedabber od devarekha — David's response: why do you keep speaking? Amarti: attah ve-Tziva tachlequ et-ha-sadeh — “you and Ziba shall divide the field.” David's compromise verdict — splitting the inheritance between Ziba and Mephibosheth — implicitly acknowledges that his original grant to Ziba (16:4) was made on insufficient evidence, but he lacks enough confidence in Mephibosheth's counter-narrative to fully reverse it.
30: Vayomer Mephiboshet el-ha-melekh: gam et-ha-kol yiqach — Mephibosheth's response: “let him take everything.” Gam et-ha-kol yiqach (even let him take everything) — the renunciation of the property claim in favor of the king's shalom: acharei asher-ba adoni ha-melekh be-shalom el-beito (after my lord the king has come home in peace). Whether interpreted as genuine loyalty, political wisdom, or resignation, the renunciation echoes the legal waiver-of-claim gesture.
31: Ve-Barzillai ha-Gil'adi yarad mi-Rogelim va-ya'avor et-ha-Yarden im-ha-melekh le-shal'cho min-ha-Yarden — Barzillai the Gileadite escorting David across the Jordan. The verb le-shal'cho (to send him off/escort him) is the farewell-escort idiom — the same idiom used for honorable departures in Gen 18:16 and Ruth. Barzillai is very old (ben-shemonim shanah, eighty years old) and very wealthy.
32: Ve-hu kilkel et-ha-melekh be-shivto be-Machanayim — Barzillai had supported David during the Mahanaim exile (kilkel, Pilel of kul, to sustain, provision). Ki ish gadol hu me'od — for he was a very great man. The portrait of Barzillai — old, wealthy, great — depicts a man at the pinnacle of social position who gave material support to a fallen king at personal risk.
33: Vayomer ha-melekh el-Barzillai: attah avor itti ve-kilkalti otekha imadi vi-Yerushalaim — David's invitation: cross over with me and I will sustain you (kilkalti, the same verb Barzillai used for his provisioning of David). The reciprocal use of kilkel creates a verbal contract of mutual provision.
34: Vayomer Barzillai el-ha-melekh: kamah yemei shenei chayai ki-e'eleh im-ha-melekh Yerushalaim — “how many years of my life remain that I should go up with the king to Jerusalem?” Barzillai's self-assessment — ben-shemonim shanah anokhi ha-yom (I am eighty years old today) — frames his refusal as a practical assessment of what old age cannot enjoy, not ingratitude.
35: Ha-eda od ha-tov ve-ha-ra' — “can I still discern good from evil?” The question inverts the formula applied to the king as ideal judge (14:17, lishmo'a ha-tov ve-ha-ra'). Barzillai admits the faculties of wisdom and pleasure have diminished. Im-yita'em avdekha et-asher yokhal ve-et-asher eshte im-eshma od be-qol sharim ve-sharot — can I taste what I eat and drink? Can I hear the voice of singers and singing women?
36: Me'at ya'avor avdekha et-ha-Yarden im-ha-melekh — “your servant will go a short way (me'at) with the king over the Jordan.” The partial escort reflects Barzillai's compromise: accompany David to the Jordan but not to Jerusalem. Ve-lammah yigmel ha-melekh li ha-gemul ha-zeh — why should the king recompense me with this recompense?
37: Yashuv na avdekha ve-amut be-iri im-qever avi ve-immi — Barzillai's request: let me die in my city, near the graves of my father and mother. The desire to die in one's ancestral city and be buried near parents reflects the ancient Near Eastern attachment to ancestral burial sites. Ve-hinneh avdekha Kimham ya'avor im-adoni ha-melekh — but here is your servant Chimham; let him cross with my lord the king.
38: Vayomer ha-melekh: itti ya'avor Kimham ve-ani e'eseh-lo et-ha-tov asher-tiv beineykhah alai — David grants the substitute: Chimham (likely Barzillai's son) will cross with David and receive the promised favor. The phrase asher-tiv beineykhah alai (whatever you desire from me) is an open-ended royal gift formula.
39: Va-yishaq ha-melekh le-Varzillai va-yevarekhehu va-yashov limqomo — David kissed Barzillai (the kiss of formal farewell-honor) and blessed him, and Barzillai returned to his place. The departure of Barzillai with his blessing is one of the Succession Narrative's most affecting scenes: the wealthy old man who asked for nothing except permission to die at home.
40: Va-ya'avor ha-melekh ha-Gilgal ve-Kimham avar imo — David crossing to Gilgal with Chimham. Ve-khol-am Yehudah he'eviru et-ha-melekh ve-gam chtzi am Yisra'el — all the people of Judah crossed the king over, and also half the people of Israel. The half-and-half division foreshadows the tribal dispute that immediately follows.
41: Ve-hinneh kol-anshei Yisra'el ba'u el-ha-melekh va-yomeru el-ha-melekh: maddua genevekhah achinu anshei Yehudah va-ya'aviru et-ha-melekh — “why have our brothers, the men of Judah, stolen you (genevekhah) and brought the king over?” The northern tribes' accusation uses ganav (steal) — the third deployment of this root in the Succession Narrative (15:6, 19:3, now 19:41). The tribal jealousy that will eventually split the kingdom (1 Kgs 12) appears here in embryo.
42: Vaya'an kol-ish Yehudah al-ish Yisra'el: ki-qarov ha-melekh elai — Judah's answer: because the king is my kinsman (qarov). The kinship argument is both the claim and the problem — Judah asserts special access to the king on the basis of tribal proximity. Ve-lammah zeh charah lakhem al-ha-davar ha-zeh — why be angry about this? We ate nothing of the king's; was any gift given to us?
43: Vaya'an ish Yisra'el et-ish Yehudah vayomer: eser yadot li ba-melekh — the northern rejoinder: “I have ten shares in the king.” Eser yadot (ten hands/shares) reflects the ten-tribe majority's claim to greater proportional stake in the king. Ve-gam be-David ani mimkha — “and also in David I am more than you.” The tribal arithmetic dispute — ten vs. one, proportional representation vs. kinship priority — is a constitutional argument about the nature of Davidic kingship that has no resolution within the Succession Narrative.
